---
title: ""
format: 
  revealjs: 
    height: 900
    slide-level: 3
    link-external-newwindow: true
    chalkboard: true
    controls: true
    auto-stretch: false
    code-overflow: wrap
tbl-colwidths: [50,50]
---

# "Statistical Significance"

### So Far : Prediction (& Error) in Our Dataset {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
There is a relationship between age and narcissism (b = -.06). Older people (IN OUR DATASET) report being lower in narcissism than younger people (IN OUR DATASET).

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 5

mba <- read.csv("~/Dropbox/!WHY STATS/Chapter Datasets/Hormone Data/hormone_dataset.csv", stringsAsFactors = T)
age.mod <- lm(NPI ~ age, data = mba)
par(mfrow = c(1,1))
plot(NPI ~ age, data = mba, pch = 19, xlab = "Age", ylab = "Narcissism (NPI)")
abline(age.mod, lwd = 5, col = 'red')
round(coef(age.mod), 2)
```
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
**Question : can we use the information IN OUR DATASET to make a claim about PEOPLE IN GENERAL????**
:::
:::::

### Inferential Statistics : Population and Sample {.smaller}

**Goal : use some data to learn about *a larger population*?**

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**the population :** all the individuals who might be relevant to your research question. ![](images/clipboard-1823214230.png)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**the sample :** the individuals who the researcher actually studies.

![](images/clipboard-4127019705.png)
:::
:::::

### Sample Bias and Error {.smaller}

**Problem : our data are unlikely to perfectly represent the general population (so need to estimate influence of each.)**

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**sampling bias** = predictable mistakes in sampling.

-   critical thinking skills

-   see if slope *depends on* another variable.

**sampling error** = random mistakes in sampling.

-   ~~run the study many many times~~

-   make up some numbers using statistics to estimate sampling error
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
[![Lima-Oliveira, G., Guidi, G. C., Salvagno, G. L., Danese, E., Montagnana, M., & Lippi, G. (2017). Patient posture for blood collection by venipuncture: recall for standardization after 28 years. Revista brasileira de hematologia e hemoterapia, 39(2), 127–132. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bjhh.2017.01.004](images/clipboard-3239626908.png){fig-align="center"}](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5457469/)
:::
:::::

### Sample Bias and Scientific Lying

![](images/clipboard-864377562.png){fig-align="center" width="60%"}

## Inferential Statistics : standard error, t-value, and the p-value. {.smaller}

::::::::::::::: panel-tabset
### 1. Slope (from our data!) {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 5
mba <- read.csv("~/Dropbox/!WHY STATS/Chapter Datasets/Hormone Data/hormone_dataset.csv", stringsAsFactors = T)
age.mod <- lm(NPI ~ age, data = mba)
coef(age.mod)
par(mfrow = c(1,1))
plot(NPI ~ age, data = mba, pch = 19, xlab = "Age", ylab = "Narcissism (NPI)")
abline(age.mod, lwd = 5, col = 'red')
```
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
```{r}
summary(age.mod)
```
:::
:::::

### 2. Standard Error {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 5

# Generate points for the t-distribution
df <- 106
x <- seq(-4, 4, length=200)
y <- dt(x, df)

# Plot the distribution
plot(x, y, type="l", lwd=2, col="blue", 
     main=paste("If Null, Expected Slope = 0 \n(but expect each sample to vary)"),
     ylab="Density (Total Probability)", xlab="Distribution of Sample Estimates (T-Distribution)")
abline(v = c(-.022,.022), lty = "dashed")
```

$$
\text{standard error (SE)} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$


:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
```{r}
summary(age.mod)
```
:::
:::::

### 3. t-value {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 5

p_value <- 0.0122
df <- 106
alpha <- p_value 
# Calculate critical t-values for two-sided
t_crit <- qt(1 - alpha/2, df)

# Generate points for the t-distribution
x <- seq(-4, 4, length=200)
y <- dt(x, df)

# Plot the distribution
plot(x, y, type="l", lwd=2, col="blue", 
     main=paste("T-Value = -.05 / .02 = -2.55"),
     ylab="Density (Total Probability)", xlab="T-Distribution")
abline(v=-t_crit, col="darkred", lwd=2, lty=2)
```

$$
t = \frac{b}{SE}
$$


:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
```{r}
summary(age.mod)
```
:::
:::::

### 4. p-value {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 5

p_value <- 0.0122
df <- 106
alpha <- p_value 

# Calculate critical t-values for two-sided
t_crit <- qt(1 - alpha/2, df)

# Generate points for the t-distribution
x <- seq(-4, 4, length=200)
y <- dt(x, df)

# Plot the distribution
plot(x, y, type="l", lwd=2, col="blue", 
     main=paste("Two-Sided P-value =", p_value, "| df =", df),
     ylab="Density (Total Probability)", xlab="T-Distribution")

# Shade the right tail
x_right <- seq(t_crit, 4, length=100)
y_right <- dt(x_right, df)
polygon(c(t_crit, x_right, 4), c(0, y_right, 0), col="red", border=NA)

# Shade the left tail
x_left <- seq(-4, -t_crit, length=100)
y_left <- dt(x_left, df)
polygon(c(-4, x_left, -t_crit), c(0, y_left, 0), col="red", border=NA)

# Add t-score lines
abline(v=c(-t_crit, t_crit), col="darkred", lwd=2, lty=2)
axis(1, at=c(-t_crit, t_crit), labels=c(round(-t_crit, 2), round(t_crit, 2)))
```

$p = Pr(Ts \geq |t_{\text{obs}}| \mid H_0)$


:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
```{r}
summary(age.mod)
```
:::
:::::
:::::::::::::::

### TLDR. {.smaller}

1.  We estimate how much sampling error there might be (if the null hypothesis were "true").
2.  We compare the slope we found in our one study to this guess about sampling error.
3.  If t-value is high (and p-value is low) then our slope is VERY DIFFERENT from sampling error we would expect if the null were true and we can reject the null hypothesis.
4.  This is all made up : we might be making a mistake AND a "significant effect" doesn't mean large or important.

## Read More : Chapter on Inferential Statistics in *Why Statistics?*

![](images/clipboard-2891465347.png)
